Facts.text: Roman military commander Avitus is proclaimed emperor of the Western Roman Empire
Battle of Hwangsanbeol: At Nonsan, China a 50,000 strong Silla force led by general Kim Yu-shin annihilate the army of Baekje and kill their leader general Gyebaek
18 Rabbit [Yax K'uk Mo'] ascends to the throne of the important Maya city state of Copán and reigns for 43 years
Berbers under Tarik-ibn Ziyad occupy North Spain
Charles IV, Holy Roman emperor, assists laying the foundation stone of Charles Bridge in Prague
Pope Gregory XI names Arnold II of Horne as bishop of Utrecht
Battle at Sempach: Swiss beat Duke Leopold III of Austria
Gelderse crowd robber murders population of Asperen

Estevão da Gama departs Massawa, leaving behind 400 matchlock men and 150 slaves under his brother Christovão da Gama, with orders to help the Emperor of Ethiopia defeat Ahmad ibn Ibrihim al-Ghazi who has invaded his Empire
Battle at Sievershausen: Saxons-Brunwick alliance led by Elector Maurice defeat forces of Margrave Albert Alcibiades of Brandenburg-Bayreuth in a bloody battle that kills 4,000
Death of the Gorcum Martyrs: Calvinist militant force the Watergeuzen (the Sea Beggars) capture and hang 19 Catholic priests in Brielle
Emperor Rudolf II grants Bohemian protestants freedom of religion
Germany, Sweden, and Spain sign anti-French League of Augsburg
Battle of Fort Duquesne (modern Pittsburgh): French troops repel a British advance
British General Edward Braddock mortally wounded during Battle of Fort Duquesne during French and Indian War
British premier Marquess of Rockingham resigns
Denmark declares neutrality, joining First League of Armed Neutrality to protect shipping
French Revolution: the National Assembly renames itself the National Constituent Assembly
James Swan pays off the $2,024,899 US national debt
Mt Vernon Gardens becomes site of first summer theater in US
Captain John B. Montgomery claims Yerba Buena (San Francisco) for US
The territory of the District of Columbia south of the Potomac River (39 mi² or about 100 km²) is returned to Virginia through an Act of Congress
Fire destroys 1,100 construction sites in Montreal, Canada
Commodore Matthew Perry and four US Navy vessels visit Japan to force them to open up to American trade and end their policy of isolation
Temperature hits 115°F in Fort Scott and 112°F in Topeka, Kansas
Confederate GeneralJohn Hunt Morganbegins "Morgan's Raid", his cavalry raid into Indiana by leading 2,100 men across the Ohio river
US Union troops enter Port Hudson, Louisiana, gaining control of the Mississippi River
Battle of Monocacy, Maryland, Confederate forces led by Lieutenant General Jubal Early
The first British train murder: bank clerk Thomas Briggs is robbed and killed by German tailor Franz Muller on the North London Railway. Muller is later chased to NY, tried, and executed.
Francis L. Cardozo becomes the first African American cabinet member in South Carolina as Secretary of State
Louisiana and South Carolina are the last states to ratify the 14th Amendment to the United States Constitution, guaranteeing civil rights
Donut cutter patents granted to John Blondel, Thomaston, Maine
American inventor Henry Tibbe patents an improved corncob pipe design
The Commonwealth of Australia is established by the British House of Commons
CHU (Christian Historic Union) Dutch political party forms
Walter Brookins, flying a Wright biplane over Atlantic City, New Jersey, becomes first to fly an airplane to an altitude of 1 mile (actually reaching 6,175 feet or 1.169 miles)
First US duplicate auction bridge championship held in Lake Placid, NY
Germany surrenders South West Africa to Union of South Africa
First cargo submarine to cross Atlantic arrives in US from Germany
101 killed and 171 injured in worst US train wreck in Nashville, Tennessee
US Congress creates Distinguished Service Medal (not to be confused with other countries' decorations of the same name)
General Sinel de Cordes, one of the leaders of May 28 coup d'état, in Portugal becomes Minister of Finance
William T. Francis named US minister to Liberia
The state of São Paulo revolts against the Brazilian federal government, starting the Constitutionalist Revolution
Washington Redskins (then Boston Braves) formed
Yankees' Ben Chapman hits two inside-the-park home runs, tying the record
Philadelphia-based NFL team Frankford Yellow Jackets is sold and rebranded as Philadelphia Eagles
A meeting of 6,000 Indians, held at the Indian Sports Ground in Johannesburg, South Africa, launches the Passive Resistance Campaign against apartheid and racial policy in South Africa
German Evangelist Church protests against euthanasia pogroms
Dutch-American Physicist Abraham Pais is awarded his Ph.D in the Netherlands five days before a Nazi deadline banning Jews from receiving degrees
British air raid sinks U-435
Fifth day of Battle of Kursk, USSR: Operation Citadel - Germans occupy Verkhopenye
In World War II, US troops secure Saipan as Japan falls
U-740 is sunk by depth charges from a British Liberator aircraft
13.15 inches (33.40 cm) of rainfall in York, Nebraska (state 24-hour record)
First helicopter passenger service in New York City
Phillies Robin Roberts ends streak of 28 consecutive complete games
E. Frederic Morrow is the first African American executive on US White House staff
Strike in Belgium for five-day workweek
Giant splash caused by the fall of 90 million tons of rock and ice into Lituya Bay, Alaska, washes 1,800 feet up the mountain
Portuguese cargo ship "Save" explodes after catching fire and running aground off coast of Mozambique; over 250 crew members are killed
US performs atmospheric nuclear test at Johnston Island
Crusher Lisowski defeats Verne Gagne in Minneapolis to become NWA champion
John Edrich completes 310* in a cricket Test match against New Zealand in 532 minutes, hitting 52 fours and 5 sixes
Senators' Frank Howard ties record with seven strikeouts in a doubleheader
WRET TV channel 36 in Charlotte, North Carolina (NBC/CBS) begins broadcasting
15.68 inches (39.83 cm) of rainfall in Columbus, Mississippi (state 24-hour record)
BBC-TV broadcasts "Farewell," folk-rock band The Seekers' final performance, drawing 10 million viewers
In Atlanta, Chief-No-ka-homa is joined by cousin Chief Round-the-Horn
Springhill massacre: British snipers shoot dead five Catholic civilians and wound two others in Springhill, Belfast
The ceasefire between the Provisional IRA and the British Army comes to an end
USSR performs underground nuclear test
Ninth Maccabiah Games open in Tel Aviv, Israel
The National Assembly of Senegal passes a law that paves way for a (albeit highly restricted) multi-party system
England all out for 71 against West Indies at Old Trafford, with Holding taking 5 wickets for 17 runs in 14 overs
Houston Astro Larry Dierker no-hits Montreal Expos, 6-0
Uganda asks UN to condemn Israeli hostage rescue raid on Entebbe, but it does not
American Nazi Party holds a rally at Marquette Park, Chicago
Nearly 100,000 demonstrators march on Washington, D.C. for ERA
A car bomb destroys a Renault owned by famed "Nazi hunters" Serge and Beate Klarsfeld at their home in France. A note purportedly from ODESSA claims responsibility.
Dr Walter Massey named director of Argonne National Laboratory
Jacksons begin a 36-city tour
Botham scores 208 in 225 balls in the England vs. India match at The Oval
Michael Fagan breaks into Buckingham Palace and enters the Queen's bedroom in Buckingham Palace, London
Pan Am Boeing 727 crashes in a storm in Kenner, Louisiana, shortly after take-off, killing 153
12th-century York Minster damaged in lightning storm
South African police arrest Dutch ANC member Klaas de Jong
Attorney General's Commission on pornography links hard-core porn to sex crimes
Padres trade pitcher Tim Stoddard to Yankees for pitcher Ed Whitson
The New Zealand parliament passes the Homosexual Law Reform Bill legalising consensual sex between men aged 16 and older
One million South Koreans demonstrate against Chun Doo Hwan regime
Chris Speier hits for the cycle, and Ernest Riles hits the 10,000th home run for the Giants
Two bombs explode in Mecca, killing 1 pilgrim and wounding 16 others
South Africa re-admitted to Olympics
Space Shuttle STS 50 (Columbia 13) lands
11,000th home run in New York Yankees history (Matt Nokes)
Russian spacecraft Soyuz TM-18 lands after successful trip to Mir spacestation
Sonia O'Sullivan runs a 2000 m world record in 5:25.36
US international postage rates rise to 60 cents per ounce
US Senate approves 90 cent raise to $4.25 minimum wage
Baseball's Triple-A American Association (formed in 1902) votes to disband
Days of student protests begin after Iranian police and hardliners attack a student dormitory at the University of Tehran
Police fire tear gas at fans during a World Cup qualifying soccer game between Zimbabwe and South Africa, setting off a stampede that kills 12 people in Harare, Zimbabwe
At least 122 people killed after a Sibir Airlines Airbus A310 passenger jet carrying 200 passengers veers off the runway while landing at Irkutsk Airport in Siberia in wet conditions
FIFA World Cup Final, Olympiastadion, Berlin, Germany: Italy beats France, 5-3 on penalties; 1-1 after extra time
South Sudan gains independence and secedes from Sudan.
Super Rugby Final, Suncorp Stadium, Brisbane: Queensland Reds beat Canterbury Crusaders 18-13 at home for their first Super Rugby title
Spokesperson for Medical Aid for Palestinians claims that at least seven children have been killed by Israeli air strikes
Typhoon Rammasun, one of only three known Category Five Super Typhoons, forms in the South China Sea and goes on to kill 151 people
Chuck Blazer receives a lifetime ban from FIFA and all football related activity due to corruption
Hundreds of thousands protest against the Turkish government at a rally in Istanbul, Turkey
Thai amateur Atthaya Thitikul becomes youngest winner in a female professional golf event at 14 years, 4 months, and 19 days at Ladies European Thailand Championship
Bright pink is the world's oldest biological color according to scientists, produced by tiny cyanobacteria in 1.1 billion-year-old marine shale rock from the Taoudeni Basin, Mauritania
Four more boys are rescued from Tham Luang Nang Non cave, Thailand, after being trapped there with eight others and their coach for 17 days by monsoon flooding (four boys and their coach remain)
Hedge fund billionaire David Tepper buys Carolina Panthers for an NFL record $2.275 billion
Train derailment kills 24 in the Turkish province of Tekirdag
US coffee company Starbucks announces it will stop using plastic straws by 2020, reducing use of more than 1 billion straws a year
Indian restaurateur "Dosa King" P. Rajagopal finally begins his life sentence for organizing a murder 15 years after he was convicted
Northern Ireland MPs vote to extend same-sex marriage and make abortion legal to bring it in line with rest of UK
McGirt v. Oklahoma, in landmark case, US Supreme Court rules that Congress has not disestablished Oklahoma Indian reserves
Death Valley, California, hits a temperature of 130 degrees F (54.4 C), one of the highest temperatures ever recorded on earth
June 2021 is declared the hottest June ever recorded in the US in 127 years, with an average temperature of 72.6 degrees F (4.2 degrees above average)
Russian airstrike on a residential building in Chasiv Yar, eastern Ukraine, kills at least 47

Thousands of Sri Lankan protesters storm the Presidential Palace and set the Prime Minister's house on fire in Colombo, leading to promises that both leaders will resign amid continuing economic turmoil
Ariane 6, the European Space Agency's new heavy-lift rocket, blasts off from French Guiana
At least 29 Palestinians are killed in an Israeli missile attack on a displaced persons camp and school in Abasan al-Kabira, Gaza
Turko-Mongol rulerTamerlane(Timur) destroys Baghdad, killing 20,000 people
French navigatorJacques Cartierreturns to Saint-Malo, France, from his exploration of what is now Canada
William IIItakes the oath of stadholder of the States of Holland
French ships carryingBonnie Prince Charlieto Scotland, the Du Teillay and the Elizabeth, battle HMS Lion, eventually escaping with 107 killed on both sides

KingGustav IIIleads Swedish naval forces to victory over the Russian Baltic fleet during the Second Battle of Svensksund, destroying or capturing one-third of the Russian ships and ending the Russo-Swedish War
Napoleonannexes the Kingdom of Holland as part of the First French Empire
Charles Maurice deTalleyrand-Périgord takes office as the first Prime Minister of France
KingLouis XVIIIleaves Ghent for France
Argentina declares independence from Spain at theCongress of Tucumán
Confederate GeneralJohn Hunt Morgancaptures Tompkinsville, Kentucky
An unsuccessful expedition led by E.D Young sets out to search for DrDavid Livingstone(Scottish missionary and explorer)

SurgeonDaniel Hale Williamsperforms the first successful open-heart surgery when he repairs the torn pericardium of knife wound patient James Cornish, without the use of penicillin or blood transfusion
Boston Red Sox purchase future Baseball Hall of Fame sluggerBabe Ruth's contract from minor league Baltimore Orioles
Chiang Kai-shekappointed to national-revolutionary supreme commander in China
SS-ReichsfuehrerHeinrich Himmlertakes command of German concentration camps
Engagement of Britain'sPrincess Elizabethand LieutenantPhilip Mountbatten
Satchel Paige, 42, debuts in majors, pitching two scoreless innings for Cleveland in St. Louis
US PresidentHarry Trumanasks Congress to formally end state of war with Germany
Bill Haley& His Comets' "Rock Around the Clock" tops the Billboard chart as one of the best-selling singles ever
The Russell-Einstein Manifesto is released byBertrand Russellin London on the need to avoid nuclear war
Dick Clark's first appearance as host of American Bandstand
Discovery of element 102 (Nobelium) announced by an international team at Nobel Institute of Physics in Stockholm, named afterAlfred Nobel
Andy Warhol's first West Coast gallery exhibition in the Ferus Gallery of Los Angeles
Wilt Chamberlainbecomes the first reigning NBA MVP to be traded the next season when he moves from Philadelphia 76'ers to LA Lakers
Tom Seaver's no-hit bid against the Cubs ends with one out in the ninth inning
National Security AdvisorHenry Kissingersecretly visits the People's Republic of China to negotiate a detente between the US and China
First tour of France byPaul McCartneyand Wings
Kwame Nkrumahis re-buried in Nkroful in his native Ghana
Pierre Trudeau's Liberal Party wins Canadian parliamentary election
Dutch war criminalPieter Mentensentenced to 10 years in prison
Seven people die in a stampede to see PopeJohn Paul IIin Brazil
Nintendo releases arcade game "Donkey Kong," created byShigeru Miyamoto, in Japan (July 31 in the US)
Colombian drug traffickerGriselda Blancoconvicted in New York of manufacturing, smuggling, and distributing cocaine and sentenced to 15 years
Atlanta'sDale Murphydoesn't play, ending his consecutive streak at 740
Columbia Records releases "The Bridge," singer-songwriterBilly Joel's 10th studio album
ColonelOliver Northadmits to shredding Iran-Contra evidence
Jessye Normanbegins recordingBizet's "Carmen"
Nolan Ryanbecomes the seventh player to win 100 games with two teams as the Astros beat the Mets 6-3
Kim Basingergets 1,959th star on Hollywood's Walk of Fame

Former South African PresidentF. W. de Klerkis accused of knowing about and condoning a "dirty tricks" campaign waged against the ANC between 1990 and the 1994 election in a bid to destabilize the organization
Jerry Garcia's unexpected final concert with The Grateful Dead takes place at Soldier Field, Chicago, Illinois
"Blue" debut album byLeAnn Rimesis released (Billboard Album of the Year, 1997)
Coming of age film "American Pie" is released, directed by Paul Weitz, starring Jason Biggs,Eugene Levy, and an ensemble cast
The mockumentary "The Office," created byRicky Gervaisand Stephen Merchant and starring Ricky Gervais andMartin Freeman, premieres on BBC Two in the UK
The African Union is established in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia. The first chairman isThabo Mbeki, President of South Africa.
Joe Sakicretires after 21 NHL seasons with the Quebec Nordiques/Colorado Avalanche franchise, finishing with 625 goals and 1,641 points
Adam Ondraonsights Cabane au Canada in Switzerland, the second-ever onsight of a 9a (5.14d) route
Joko Widodois elected President of Indonesia
CEOElon Muskis the first owner of Telsa's first mass-market electric car, the Model 3
English international footballerWayne Rooneyannounces he will leave Manchester United after 13 years to rejoin his original club, Everton
Iraqi Prime MinisterHaider al-Abadiproclaims victory over Islamic State forces in Mosul
Eritrean President Isaias Afewerki and Ethiopia's Prime MinisterAbiy Ahmedofficially end the "no war, no peace" peace deal made in 2000 in Eritrea's capital, Asmara
Jeremy Hunt is appointed UK Foreign Secretary afterBoris Johnsonresigns in protest afterTheresa Maytakes a softer Brexit stance
US PresidentDonald TrumpnamesBrett Kavanaughas his Supreme Court nominee
Fantasy comedy film "Barbie," starringMargot RobbieandRyan Goslingand directed byGreta Gerwig, premieres at the Shrine Auditorium in Los Angeles
US Treasury SecretaryJanet Yellenand Chinese economic leaders end 2 days of talks in Beijing, aimed at stabilizing relations between the world's two biggest economies
US Declaration of Independence is read to George Washington's troops in New York
Second Treaty of Tilsit signed by France, Russia, and Prussia
First Wimbledon tennis championship - first official lawn tennis tournament - begins featuring men's singles only
British battleship HMS Vanguard explodes at Scapa Flow due to an internal explosion caused by faulty cordite, killing 804 crew members
Voyager 2 flies past Jupiter
Kameyama, 90th Emperor of Japan (1260-74), born in Japan (d. 1305)
Dorothea of Saxe-Lauenburg, Queen of Denmark and Norway, born in Lauenburg Castle, Germany (d. 1571)
John van Hembyze, Flemish Calvinist, born in Ghent, Belgium (d. 1584)
Thomas West, 3rd & 12th Baron De La Warr, English Jamestown colonist and Governor of Virginia, born in Wherwell, Hampshire, England (d. 1618)
John of Saint Thomas [João Poinsot], Portuguese Dominican friar, theologist, and philosopher, born in Lisbon, Portugal (d. 1644)
Reigen [Satohito], 112th Emperor of Japan (1663-87), born in Japan (d. 1732)
Alexis Piron, French dramatist (La Métromanie), born in Dijon, France (d. 1773)
Johann Nikolaus Götz, German poet (Die Mädcheninsel), born in Worms, Germany (d. 1781)
William Waldegrave, 1st Baron Radstock, Governor of Newfoundland, born in England (d. 1825)
Ann Radcliffe, English Gothic novelist (The Mysteries of Udolpho, The Romance of the Forest), born in London (d. 1823)
Jacob Perkins, American inventor (father of the refrigerator), born in Newburyport, Massachusetts (d. 1849)
Johanna Schopenhauer, German writer and mother of Arthur Schopenhauer, born in Gdańsk, Royal Prussia, Crown of the Kingdom of Poland (d. 1838)
Matthew Lewis, English novelist (The Monk), born in London (d. 1818)
Henry Hallam, British lawyer and historian who wrote on European history, born in Windsor, England (d. 1859)
Sophie Hélène Béatrix, Princess of France, daughter of Louis XVI of France and Marie Antoinette, born in Palace of Versailles, France (d. 1787)
Nicolás Ledesma, Spanish organist and composer, born in Grisel, Spain (d. 1883)
Friedrich Gustav Jakob Henle, German pathologist, born in Fürth, Bavaria (d. 1885)
Thomas Davenport, American inventor (invented 1st commercial electric motor), born in Williamstown, Vermont (d. 1851)
Henry John Gauntlett, English organist and composer, born in Wellington, Shropshire (d. 1876)
Alexander William Doniphan, American lawyer and soldier, born in Mason County, Kentucky (d. 1887)
Jules Oppert, French-German Assyriologist (decodes characters), born in Hamburg, Germany (d. 1905)
Luigi Oreglia di Santo Stefano, Italian Catholic churchman and last surviving cardinal of Pius IX, born in Bene Vagienna, Italy (d. 1913)
Johan Philip van der Kellen, Dutch stamp cutter, lithographer and writer, born in Utrecht, Netherlands (d. 1906)
Camille de Renesse, Belgian nobleman and entrepreneur, born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1904)
Carl Baermann, German composer, born in Munich, Germany (d. 1913)
Carl Lumbye, Danish composer, born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1911)
Robert I, Duke of Parma, last ruling Duke of Parma, born in Florence, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (now Italy) (d. 1907)
Paul Zilcher, German pianist, composer, and piano teacher, born in Frankfurt am Main, Germany (d. 1943)
Daniel Guggenheim, American mining magnate and philanthropist, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1930)
Frederick II, Grand Duke of Baden (1907-18), born in Karlsruhe, Grand Duchy of Baden (d. 1928)
Carlos Chagas, Brazilian sanitary physician, born in Oliveira, Minas Gerais, Empire of Brazil (d. 1934)
Friedrich Adler, Austrian socialist politician, revolutionary and murderer of premier Storgkh, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1960)
Ottorino Respighi, Italian composer (Pines of Rome), born in Bologna, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1936)
Richard Hageman, Dutch born American pianist, composer and conductor (Caponsacchi), born in Leeuwarden, Netherlands (d. 1966)
Samuel Eliot Morison, American historian (Admiral of the Ocean Sea), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1976)
Léo Dandurand, American-Canadian hockey executive, born in Bourbonnais, Illinois (d. 1964)
George Geary, English cricket fast bowler (14 Tests, 46 wickets), born in Barwell, Leicestershire, England (d. 1981)
Pyotr Kapitsa, Russian physicist (Nobel Prize 1978), born in Kronshtadt, Russian Empire (d. 1984)
(James) Cullen Landis, American silent and sound screen actor (Soul of the Beast; Peacock Feathers), and director, born in Nashville, Tennessee (d. 1975)
Gerard Walschap, Flemish writer (Mary, Revolt in Congo), born in Londerzeel, Belgium (d. 1989)
Marcel Delannoy, French composer, born in La Ferté-Alais, Essonne, France (d. 1962)
Robert Oboussier, Swiss composer, born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1957)
Barbara Cartland, English romance author (Camfield #69), born in Edgbaston, Birmingham, England (d. 2000)
Jester Hairston, American actor (That's My Mama, Amen), born in Belews Creek, North Carolina (d. 2000)
Gerhard Pohl, German writer (Crazy Ferdinand), born in Trachenberg, Silesia (d. 1966)
Myles Horton, American educator and Civil Rights Movement teacher, born in Savannah, Tennessee (d. 1990)
Beene Dubbelboer, Dutch writer (Secret Resistance), born in Tweede Exloërmond, Netherlands (d. 1982)
Joe Darensbourg, Cajun-American dixieland-style jazz clarinetist (Jelly Roll Morton; Louis Armstrong), born in Baton Rouge, Louisiana (d. 1985)
Walter Sande, American actor (To Have and Have Not), born in Denver, Colorado (d. 1971)
Eddie Dean [Glosup], American cowboy singer ("One Has My Name (The Other Has My Heart)", "Way Out Yonder"), and actor (Harmony Trail), born in Posey, Texas (d. 1999)
Rasheed Turabi, Pakistani scholar, orator and philosopher, born in Hyderabad, India (d. 1973)
Harold Fox, American fashion designer (the zoot suit), and big band trumpeter, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1996)
John Archibald Wheeler, American physicist (first American involved in the theoretical development of the atomic bomb), born in Jacksonville, Florida (d. 2008)
Mervyn Peake, British writer and illustrator (Gormenghast books), born in Lushan District, Jiangxi, China (d. 1968)
Simon Fraser [Shimi], Lord Lovat, Scottish 25th head of clan Fraser and cattle breeder, born in Inverness, Scotland (d. 1995)
Willi Stoph, German politician (Prime Minister of German Democratic Republic 1964-73 and 1976-89), born in Schöneberg, Berlin, Germany (d. 1999)
David Diamond, American composer (Paderewski Prize-1943), born in Rochester, New York (d. 2005)[1]
June Richmond, American jazz singer (Jimmy Dorsey; Cab Calloway; Andy Kirk - "Hey Lawdy Mama"), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1962)[1]
Dean Goffin, New Zealand composer, born in Wellington, New Zealand (d. 1984)
Joe Liggins [Theodro Elliott], American jazz, R&B, and jump blues pianist, songwriter, and vocalist ("The Honeydripper"; "Pink Champagne"), born in Seminole, Oklahoma (d. 1987)
Ted Steele, American orchestra leader (Cavalcade of Stars), born in Hartford, Connecticut (d. 1985)
Herbert Brün, German composer, born in Berlin, Germany (d. 2000)
Nile Kinnick Jr., American College Football Hall of Fame halfback (Heisman Trophy 1939; University of Iowa), born in Adel, Iowa (d. 1943)
Rowley Israel Arenstein, South African attorney, communist and activist (under house arrest for 18 years), born in Durban, Natal (d. 1996)
[Uppaluri Gopala] U.G. Krishnamurti, Indian philosopher and lecturer, born in Machilipatnam, British India (d 2007)

Earle Hagen, American big band trombonist (Isham Jones; Tommy Dorsey; Benny Goodman), and Emmy Award-winning television composer (The Dick Van Dyke Show; The Andy Griffith Show; I Spy; The Mod Squad; "Harlem Nocturne"), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2008)
Peggy Braithwaite, lighthouse-keeper, born on Piel Island (d. 1996)
(Alexander) "Duke" Burrell, American jazz pianist, composer, and bandleader (Louisiana Shakers; Louis Jordan; Johnny Otis), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1993)
Irv Kluger, American big band jazz and session drummer (Artie Shaw; Gramercy Five), born in New York City (d. 2006)
Jacob J. Hage, Dutch farmer and WW II resistance fighter, born in St. Maartensdijk, Netherlands (d. 1944)
Leonard Pennario, American concert pianist (Rózsa's Piano Concerto with L.A. Philharmonic), born in Buffalo, New York (d. 2008)
Pierre Cochereau, French composer, born in Paris (d. 1984)
Alan Dale [Aldo Sigismondi], American pop singer ("Cherry Pink and Apple Blossom White"), born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 2002)
Borislav Stanković, Serbian Basketball Hall of Fame administrator (International Basketball Federation secretary general 1976-2002), born in Bihać, Bosnia and Herzegovina (d. 2020)
Guru Dutt [Vasanth Padukone], Indian Bollywood actor, regarded as one of the greatest film directors of all time (Pyaasa, Kaagaz Ke Phool), born in Bangalore, Kingdom of Mysore (d. 1964)
Peter Ludwig, German businessman and art collector, born in Koblenz, Germany (d. 1996)
Tom Luken, American politician (U.S. House of Representatives from Ohio, 1974-75 and 1977-91), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 2018)
Ben Roy Mottelson, American-Danish physicist (Nobel Prize for Physics 1975 for work on structure of the atomic nucleus), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2022)[1]
Mathilde Krim, American medical researcher and founding chairman of amfAR (Foundation for AIDS Research), born in Como, Italy (d. 2018)
Murphy Anderson, American comic artist (DC Comics), born in Asheville, North Carolina (d. 2015)
Ed Ames [Edmund Dantes Urick], American popular singer (Ames Brothers - "My Cup Runneth Over"), and actor (Daniel Boone - "Mingo"), born in Malden, Massachusetts (d. 2023)[1]
Red Kelly, Canadian Hockey Hall of Fame center, coach; politician (Toronto Maple Leafs), born in Simcoe, Ontario (d. 2019)
Federico Bahamontes, Spanish road cyclist (Tour de France 1959; first to win mountain classification in all 3 Grand Tours), born in Santo Domingo-Caudilla, Spain (d. 2023)
Raúl Coloma, Chilean soccer goalkeeper (13 caps; Ferrobádminton, Municipal Santiago, Ferroviaros), born in Santiago, Chile (d. 2021)
Vince Edwards, American actor, director, and singer (Ben Casey, The Devil's Brigade, Matt Lincoln, Firehouse), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1996)

(Barton) Lee Hazlewood, American pop-country songwriter, singer, and producer ("Summer Wine"; Nancy Sinatra -"These Boots Are Made For Walkin'"; Frank and Nancy - "Something Stupid"), born in Mannford, Oklahoma (d. 2007)
Jesse McReynolds, American singer and mandolinist (Grand Ole Opry), born in Coeburn, Virginia (d. 2023)[1]
Buddy Bregman, American orchestra leader and arranger (Eddie Fisher Show; Ella Fitzgerald's Cole Porter Songbook), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2017)
Roy McLean, South African cricket batsman (40 Tests, 5 x 100, HS 142; Natal), born in Pietermaritzberg, Natal, South Africa (d. 2007)
Donald Rumsfeld, American politician (US Congress, 1963-69; Nixon Whitehouse, 1969-74; Secretary of Defense, 1975-77 & 2001-06), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2021)
Dick Orkin, American radio performer (Chickenman), and actor (Tim Conway Show), born in Williamsport, Pennsylvania (d. 2017)
Nodar Gabunia, Georgian composer, born in Tbilisi, Georgia (d. 2000)
Oliver Sacks, English neurologist and author (Awakenings), born in London (d. 2015)
Frank Wright, American free jazz saxophonist, born in Grenada, Mississippi (d. 1990)
Mercedes Sosa, Argentinian singer (exponent of nueva canción), born in San Miguel de Tucumán, Tucumán, Argentina (d. 2009)
Ronnie Burns, American actor and adopted son of George Burns, born in Evanston, Illinois (d. 2007)
Wim [Willem Frederik] Duisenberg, Dutch Minister of Finance (PvdA), born in Heerenveen, Netherlands (d. 2005)
David Zinman, American composer and conductor (Baltimore Symphony, 1985-98), born in New York City
George Cleve, Austrian-American conductor (Winnipeg Symphony, 1968-70; San Jose Symphony, 1972-92; San Francisco Midsummer Mozart Festival, 1974-2015), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 2015)
James Hampton, American actor (Bugler Dobbs-F Troop, Teen Wolf), born in Oklahoma City (d. 2021)
June Jordan, Caribbean-American playwright and poet (His Own Where), born in Harlem, New York (d. 2002)
Liisa Pohjola, Finnish concert pianist and music professor, born in Seinäjoki, Finland (d. 2021)
Barry Howard, British comic actor (Hi-de-Hi!), born in Nottingham, England (d. 2016)
Clem Daniels, American AFL and NFL football halfback,1960-68 (2 x All-Statr, AFL MVP, 1963 - Oakland Raiders; Dallas Texans; San Francisco 49ers), born in McKinney, Texas (d. 2019)
Marty Springstead, American Major League baseball umpire and umpire supervisor, born in Nyack, New York
Brian Dennehy, American actor (Check is in the Mail, F/X, Cocoon, Ants), born in Bridgeport, Connecticut (d. 2020)
Paul Seiko Chihara, American-Japanese composer, born in Seattle, Washington
Sanjeev Kumar [Harihar Jariwala], Indian Bollywood actor (Angoor, Dastak, Koshish), born in Surat, Bombay Presidency (d. 1985)
Jair da Costa, Brazilian soccer winger (1 cap; Internazionale), born in Santo André, Brazil
Manfred Jung, German operatic tenor (Wagner's heldentenor roles), born in Oberhausen, Germany (d. 2017)
Don McPherson, American R&B singer (The Main Ingredient - "Everybody Plays the Fool"), born in Indianapolis, Indiana (d. 1971)
James Scott, English filmmaker (Strike it Rich), born in Wells, England
Scotty Baesler, American politician (Rep-D-Kentucky 1993-99), born in Lexington, Kentucky
